learning_objective: Students will construct temporal visual models and causal explanations to link unobservable energy transfer and interaction forces to the observable macroscopic changes in an object's shape and motion before and after an impact.
core_ideas: In a collision between two objects, the objects have to come into contact; sometimes something is damaged, but not always.
Different factors and variables may cause objects to be damaged or not damaged in a collision.

misconceptions.a: If nothing looks damaged after the collision, the force must have been weak.
misconceptions.b: Heavier objects always cause more damage. (not thinking about other factors)

quiz_items.stem: A phone drops onto a soft pillow. The phone looks the same afterward. Which statement best describes the collision?

options.B: Only the phone pushed; the pillow did not.
options.C: The phone and pillow pushed on each other.
options.D: The force was weak because nothing was damaged.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A bug hits a car windshield while the car is moving on the highway. The windshield looks the same afterward. Which statement best describes the collision?

options.A: The bug and the windshield pushed on each other during contact.
options.B: There was no collision because the windshield did not change shape.
options.C: The windshield exerted a force, but the bug did not because it is small.
options.D: The force must have been weak because the windshield was not damaged.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?

A heavy bowling ball hits a window very slowly. A light golf ball hits the same window very fast. The bowling ball does not break the window, but the golf ball broke the window. Which statement best explain it?

options.A: Speed can matter as well as mass.
options.C: The window must have been defective.
options.D: Heavier objects always cause more damage.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
A large truck and a small compact car collide head-on. The car is badly crumpled, but the truck shows much less damage. Which statement best explains why the damage differs?

options.A: The truck must have exerted a much larger force to the car.
options.B: The car was damaged because it exerted no force on the truck.
options.C: The truck was less damaged because smaller objects have less gravity.
options.D: Damage depends on factors such as materials and design of each object, not only mass.

quiz_items.stem: How confident are you about your answer?
